showcase.extracted_claim: Creatine supplementation causes hair loss in healthy adult men.

verdict.summary: Evidence that creatine causes hair loss is weak and based largely on a single small DHT study that did not measure hair loss.
verdict.effect_size: no measurable effect on hair documented
verdict.population: studied in young men; no hair-loss outcomes
verdict.why: The 'creatine causes hair loss' claim traces back to one 2009 RCT in rugby players that found a rise in DHT, but the study did not measure hair loss itself. Subsequent reviews and meta-analyses have not replicated the DHT finding, and no trial has shown increased shedding or AGA progression.

evidence.source_title: van der Merwe et al. — Creatine and DHT in rugby players
evidence.relevance_note: Origin of the DHT claim; did not measure hair loss.

evidence.source_title: Antonio et al. — ISSN position stand: creatine safety and efficacy
evidence.relevance_note: Concludes creatine has no documented adverse effect on hair.

evidence.source_title: NIH ODS — Dietary Supplements for Exercise and Athletic Performance: Creatine
evidence.relevance_note: Government fact sheet listing established side effects; hair loss not among them.

showcase.extracted_claim: Ashwagandha increases testosterone by 40% in healthy adult men.

verdict.summary: Ashwagandha shows small testosterone increases mainly in stressed or infertile men; a 40% boost in healthy lifters is not supported.
verdict.effect_size: ~10-15% in stressed/infertile men; minimal in healthy
verdict.population: studied in stressed or infertile adult men
verdict.why: Multiple small RCTs show modest testosterone increases in stressed or sub-fertile men taking standardized ashwagandha for 8+ weeks. Effects in healthy, well-trained men are smaller and inconsistent, and no trial supports a 40% increase. The claim overstates a real but limited effect.

evidence.source_title: Lopresti et al. — Ashwagandha and hormonal balance in overweight men
Ambiye et al. — Ashwagandha in oligospermic men
evidence.relevance_note: Significant testosterone increase but in non-healthy population.

evidence.source_title: Wankhede et al. — Ashwagandha and resistance training
evidence.relevance_note: Small testosterone effect (~15%) alongside training; not 40%.

showcase.extracted_claim: BCAAs build muscle in adults eating adequate protein.

verdict.summary: BCAAs add little or no muscle growth when total daily protein intake is already adequate.
verdict.why: Muscle protein synthesis requires all essential amino acids. Studies isolating BCAAs show transient signaling effects but no advantage over whole protein. When daily protein meets ~1.6 g/kg, BCAAs add nothing measurable.

evidence.source_title: Wolfe — BCAAs do not stimulate muscle protein synthesis alone
evidence.relevance_note: Mechanistic review concluding BCAAs alone are insufficient.

evidence.source_title: Jackman et al. — BCAA ingestion and post-exercise MPS
evidence.relevance_note: BCAAs raise MPS less than whole protein.

evidence.source_title: Morton et al. — Protein intake and resistance training meta-analysis
evidence.relevance_note: Total protein, not BCAAs, drives hypertrophy.

verdict.summary: Beta-alanine moderately improves performance in efforts lasting 1-4 minutes by buffering muscle acidosis.
verdict.why: Multiple RCTs and an ISSN position stand support beta-alanine for tasks limited by muscle acidosis. Benefit is largest in the 60-240s window; effects on max strength or short sprints are smaller.

evidence.source_title: Trexler et al. — ISSN position stand on beta-alanine
evidence.relevance_note: Strong evidence for 1-4 min exercise tasks.

evidence.source_title: Saunders et al. — Beta-alanine effects updated meta

verdict.summary: Caffeine reliably improves strength and power output across many populations and dosing protocols.
verdict.why: An ISSN position stand and several meta-analyses converge on a clear, reproducible ergogenic effect of caffeine for strength, power, and endurance. Effects are robust across genotypes with modest individual variation.

evidence.source_title: Guest et al. — ISSN position stand: caffeine and exercise performance
Grgic et al. — Caffeine and muscle strength meta-analysis
Grgic — Caffeine and Wingate test meta-analysis

showcase.extracted_claim: Tongkat ali boosts testosterone 200% in healthy men.

verdict.summary: Tongkat ali shows modest testosterone restoration in hypogonadal or stressed men; a 200% increase in healthy men is not supported.
verdict.effect_size: small effect, mostly in low-T or stressed populations
verdict.population: studied mainly in older or hypogonadal men
verdict.why: Available RCTs report partial testosterone normalization in men with low baseline levels but no dramatic increases in healthy lifters. Studies are small and often industry-funded.

evidence.source_title: Tambi et al. — Tongkat ali in late-onset hypogonadism
evidence.relevance_note: Restoration toward normal range, not a 200% boost.

evidence.source_title: Talbott et al. — Tongkat ali and stress hormones
evidence.relevance_note: Modest hormonal shifts in stressed cohort.

evidence.source_title: Examine-style review of tongkat ali (NIH ODS adjacent)
evidence.relevance_note: Notes lack of large trials in healthy men.
